title: The Pharaoh Who Built His Own Tomb Twice
script: One pharaoh built his own tomb twice — and nobody can explain why. Around 1270 BC, Ramesses II ordered a second full tomb at Abydos while his real burial chamber in the Valley of the Kings was nearly finished. Two massive structures, one king, one reign. But here's the strange part — the Abydos tomb was sealed completely empty. No body, but packed with royal offerings. Archaeologists still can't agree on what he intended. Send this to someone who thinks the Egyptians always had a clear plan.
search_summary: Ramesses II built a second tomb at Abydos around 1270 BC that was sealed empty with royal offerings and no body.
comment_question: Was Ramesses II faking his own death — or planning something nobody figured out? 👀
tags: ancient history facts to fall asleep to
video_queries: valley of the kings wide shot

title: The Bird That Spreads Wildfires to Hunt
script: Some animals run from fire — these ones weaponize it. Australian Black Kites grab burning sticks and deliberately drop them ahead of a wildfire to flush out prey. Fleeing animals get snatched mid-air the second they break cover. Aboriginal Australians documented this for centuries, but science only confirmed it in 2017. But here's the part that breaks everything — they do it in coordinated groups. They learned to spread fire on their own, with zero human input. Send this to someone who still thinks tool use is a human thing. And this is how.
search_summary: Australian Black Kites deliberately carry burning sticks to spread wildfires and flush out prey, confirmed by science in 2017.
comment_question: If birds coordinate fire attacks, what else are we calling uniquely human that isn't? 🔥
source_note: Bonta et al., Journal of Ethnobiology, 2017

title: The Eye That Sees Everything But Understands Nothing
script: This creature packs 16 types of color receptors in its eyes — and still sees worse than you. The mantis shrimp holds the most complex eyes on Earth: detecting ultraviolet, infrared, and polarized light simultaneously. Humans run on three receptors. This thing has sixteen. But here's the strange part — it can't distinguish between similar shades as well as we can. All that hardware, and the brain can't fully process it. Send this to someone who thinks more is always better.
search_summary: Mantis shrimp has 16 color receptors but perceives color less accurately than humans with only 3 receptors.
comment_question: Would you trade sharper color vision for 16 types of light detection? 🦐

title: The Tiny Thing Keeping You Alive Right Now
script: Half the air you're breathing right now doesn't come from any forest. It comes from the ocean — specifically, a bacterium called Prochlorococcus, discovered in 1986. One million of them fit inside a single drop of water. Yet they produce more oxygen than the entire Amazon rainforest. But here's the wild part — it's not a plant. It's bacteria. And if it vanishes, so do we. Send this to someone who thinks trees are doing all the work. And this is how.
search_summary: Prochlorococcus bacteria discovered in 1986 produces 50% of Earth's oxygen, more than the Amazon rainforest
comment_question: So the Amazon isn't actually Earth's lungs — a microscopic bacteria is? 👀
